ecology.comments: Inhabits warm, flowing streams. Prefers rocky crevices.
ecology.biotope: Central American rivers and streams with moderate current and rocky or gravel substrates.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a conspecific school of 5+ individuals to manage natural cichlid pecking orders.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Requires high-quality flakes/pellets as a daily staple. Supplement with live or frozen foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia to copy their natural riverine diets.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat slate rocks, clay pots, or breeding caves. They lay eggs on these flat surfaces and defend them aggressively as a breeding pair.
reproduction.comments: Highly protective parents. Will guard the eggs and fry against all tankmates, making separate hatchery setups recommended.

ecology.comments: Inhabits fast-flowing streams and rivers. Grazes on driftwood.
ecology.biotope: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated tributaries of the Amazon basin, typically with stony beds and sunken wood.
ecology.socialBehavior: Generally peaceful, but males are territorial over hiding spots and caves.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Provide sinking algae wafers, fresh zucchini, spinach, and cucumber. Driftwood MUST be present in the tank as they need to ingest cellulose for proper digestion.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide narrow breeding caves, ceramic tubes, or hollow logs.
reproduction.comments: The male attracts the female to a cave, where she lays eggs. The male then guards and fans the eggs aggressively until they hatch.

vibeLine.casual: The little king that demands his own palace.
vibeLine.pro: Betta splendens. Territorial and requires warm, stable water with gentle surface access.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous and full of personality. They like having their space and will flare at their own reflection sometimes.
flavorText.pro: Best kept alone or with carefully chosen tank mates. Requires warm water (24–28°C) and access to the surface for breathing. Avoid strong currents.

hiddenLore: Magikarp from Pokémon. The ultimate underdog fish. Splash around long enough with perfect water parameters and you too might evolve into something legendary ✨
evolutionLore: Congratulations! Your Magikarp has evolved into Gyarados! The most powerful and intimidating fish in the tank. Rawr! 🐲

vibeLine.casual: The original tank buster that refuses to stay small.
vibeLine.pro: Carassius auratus. Coldwater species that grows much larger than most people expect.

flavorText.casual: Cute when small. Gets massive. Needs serious space and strong filtration. Not actually a beginner fish despite what the pet store said.
flavorText.pro: Common goldfish can live for decades and grow over 12 inches. Requires large tanks or ponds with excellent filtration. Not suitable for small aquariums long-term.

ecology.comments: Found in slow-moving streams and rice paddies.
ecology.biotope: Shallow, slow-moving streams, rice paddies, and canals in South Asia (India and Bangladesh).
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a school of 6+ to allow active shoaling and reduce stress.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Thrives on high-quality flake foods, freeze-dried bloodworms, and live daphnia. Very active surface-feeders.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Fine gravel, glass marbles substrate, or a spawning grid to prevent adults from eating the dropped eggs.
reproduction.comments: Non-adhesive eggs are scattered over the substrate. Fast and prolific spawners. Remove parent fish immediately to ensure high yield.

ecology.comments: Prefers clean, clear or blackwater streams with dense vegetation.
ecology.biotope: Shaded blackwater streams and tributaries in the western Amazon basin, rich in tannins and leaf litter.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a schooling group of 6+ (ideally 10+) individuals to reduce anxiety and display natural behavior.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Eats crushed micro-flakes, baby brine shrimp, and daphnia. Provide tiny food sizes due to their small mouth size.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense fine-leaved plants, java moss, or spawning mops under extremely dim lighting.
reproduction.comments: Eggs are highly light-sensitive and are scattered among fine vegetation. Parents should be removed immediately after spawning to prevent egg-eating.

vibeLine.casual: The classic little blue-and-red rocket that makes every tank feel alive.
vibeLine.pro: Paracheirodon innesi. Schooling species that performs best in groups of 6+ with stable soft water.

flavorText.casual: Tiny, fast, and ridiculously pretty. They look even better when they’re in a proper school. One or two just looks lonely.
flavorText.pro: Neon Tetras thrive in established, well-maintained tanks. Sensitive to ammonia and sudden parameter swings. Best kept in groups with gentle flow.

hiddenLore: Nami, the Tidecaller from League of Legends, secretly blesses this species. Keep your tank parameters perfect and she may grant you the perfect water flow ✨

ecology.comments: Highly adaptable. Found in wide range of fresh and brackish waters.
ecology.biotope: Various habitats including clear streams, canals, and brackish coastal waters of northern South America.
ecology.socialBehavior: Keep in a ratio of 1 male to 2+ females to prevent excessive harassment of females.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Enjoys spirulina-enriched flakes, freeze-dried daphnia, and micro-pellets. Needs a balance of vegetable and protein matter.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide floating plants (like Hornwort, Water Wisteria) or fry traps.
reproduction.comments: Gives birth to free-swimming live fry rather than laying eggs. Fry hide in floating vegetation to avoid predation by adults.

vibeLine.casual: The colorful, unstoppable little breeders of the hobby.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia reticulata. Extremely hardy and prolific livebearer.

flavorText.casual: Beautiful, easy to keep, and they multiply faster than you expect. Great for beginners, but be ready for babies.
flavorText.pro: One of the hardiest and most adaptable species. Excellent for new tanks and community setups, though selective breeding lines can be more delicate.

ecology.comments: Prefers fast-flowing streams and rivers with vegetation.
ecology.biotope: Fast-flowing rivers and streams with rocky bottom substrates and bankside vegetation in Central America.
ecology.socialBehavior: Avoid keeping multiple males in small tanks as they establish strict hierarchies and fight.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Eats flake food, veggie wafers, and frozen brine shrimp. Appreciates regular vegetable matter supplements.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense floating plant coverage or breeding box to protect newborn fry.
reproduction.comments: Internal fertilization occurs. Females give birth to large batches of live young. Very prolific breeders in well-planted tanks.

vibeLine.casual: The little pandas with the black eye patches.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras panda. Peaceful bottom dweller that prefers cooler, well-oxygenated water.

flavorText.casual: Adorable little armored catfish that love to hang out in groups on the bottom. Very peaceful and fun to watch.
flavorText.pro: Best kept in groups of 5+. They appreciate cooler temperatures and sandy substrate. Sensitive to poor water quality and strong medications.

ecology.comments: Found in slow-flowing rivers and ponds with rich vegetation.
ecology.biotope: Slow-flowing tributaries, oxbows, and swampy areas in the Amazon and Paraguay river basins.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a school of 6+ to minimize fin-nipping behavior directed at slower tankmates.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Accepts high-quality flake foods and small pellets. Supplement with frozen bloodworms and brine shrimp to maintain color vibrancy.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Java moss beds, spawning grids, or fine plants. Eggs adhere to the plant leaves.
reproduction.comments: Spawns in pairs or small groups. Parents are notorious egg-eaters and must be separated after the spawn event completes.

vibeLine.casual: The colorful little cichlid with main character energy.
vibeLine.pro: Mikrogeophagus ramirezi. Demands warm, soft, acidic water and pristine conditions.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous but dramatic. They like their water warm and clean, and they will let you know if they’re not happy.
flavorText.pro: Sensitive to cool temperatures and poor water quality. Best kept in pairs in well-established tanks with soft, acidic parameters around pH 6.5 and 26–28°C.

ecology.comments: Found in quiet acidic blackwater areas of the Amazon basin.
ecology.biotope: Slow-moving Amazonian tributaries and flooded forests under dense canopy cover.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a conspecific group of 6+ individuals to prevent submissive stress and establish a natural social hierarchy.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed premium beefheart mixes, freeze-dried tubifex, and frozen bloodworms. Enjoys grazing on high-protein pellets throughout the day to support body growth.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide vertical slate, breeding cones, or broad-leafed plants (e.g., Amazon Swords) for vertical egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Lays eggs on vertical surfaces. Parents produce a specialized skin mucus that serves as the primary food source for newly hatched fry.

ecology.comments: Inhabits slow-moving streams and flooded areas.
ecology.biotope: Slow-moving streams, pools, and flooded grasslands in the Paraguay river basin.
ecology.socialBehavior: Best kept in pairs or harems (1 male to multiple females) to manage territorial aggression.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Provide small frozen food like daphnia, cyclops, and baby brine shrimp. Flakes are accepted but should not be the sole diet.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide half coconut shells, small clay caves, or dense leaf litter.
reproduction.comments: Maternal mouthbrooder / cave breeder. The female guards the eggs inside the cave while the male defends the outer territory.

vibeLine.casual: Tiny galaxies swimming in your tank.
vibeLine.pro: Danio margaritatus. Peaceful schooling fish that prefers cooler, well-oxygenated water.

flavorText.casual: These little guys look like they have stars on their bodies. Super peaceful and love swimming in groups.
flavorText.pro: Best kept in schools of 6+. They prefer slightly cooler temperatures (22–24°C) and appreciate gentle flow with plenty of swimming space.

ecology.comments: Native to clearwater creeks and heavily vegetated margins of the lower Ucayali basin in Peru. Highly gregarious and social.
ecology.biotope: Clearwater creeks and heavily vegetated margins of the lower Ucayali basin

diet.fooditems: Strict biofilm and specialized soft algae grazer. Requires constant grazing surfaces or organic sinking supplements.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Strict biofilm and specialized soft algae grazer. Requires constant grazing surfaces or organic sinking supplements.

reproduction.comments: Adhesive egg layer on exposed plant substrates. Continuous breeder under highly pristine, mature conditions.

vibeLine.casual: The tiny striped vacuum that silently judges your algae situation.
vibeLine.pro: Otocinclus cocama. Highly sensitive to poor acclimation and requires established biofilm.

flavorText.casual: Looks adorable. Is actually a full-time employee. Will work tirelessly as long as there’s enough biofilm. Otherwise they will dramatically stare at you through the glass.
flavorText.pro: Prone to starvation in brand new tanks. Best introduced into mature, biofilm-rich systems. Excellent at controlling soft diatoms but not a replacement for proper husbandry.

ecology.comments: Highly adaptable freshwater dwarf shrimp. Appreciates leaf litter.
ecology.biotope: Slow-flowing streams and ponds in Taiwan, heavily vegetated with moss.
ecology.socialBehavior: Extremely peaceful. Appreciates being kept in groups.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Grazes constantly on biofilm and organic matter. Supplement with high-quality shrimp pellets or blanched vegetables.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense vegetation, mosses, and fine crevices.
reproduction.comments: Carries eggs under abdomen (berried) until they hatch into fully formed miniature shrimp.

vibeLine.casual: The tiny red janitors that make your tank feel complete.
vibeLine.pro: Neocaridina davidi. Hardy invertebrate that helps control biofilm and detritus.

flavorText.casual: Cute, busy, and surprisingly good at cleaning. They breed easily once they settle in.
flavorText.pro: Excellent cleanup crew for mature tanks. Sensitive to copper and sudden parameter changes. Best kept in groups with plenty of hiding places and biofilm.

ecology.comments: Neotenic salamander that remains aquatic throughout life. Requires cool, clean water.
ecology.biotope: Lake Xochimilco in Mexico. High altitude, cold, slow-moving water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Should be housed alone or in large tanks with same-sized axolotls. Will eat small tank mates.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed earthworms, bloodworms, or specialized axolotl pellets. Remove uneaten food quickly.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Broad-leafed plants or decorations to attach eggs.
reproduction.comments: Lays hundreds of jelly-coated eggs on plants. Cannibalistic if parents are not separated.

vibeLine.casual: The walking smile that turned the hobby into a cult.
vibeLine.pro: Ambystoma mexicanum. Cool-water amphibian that requires pristine, cold water and careful handling.

flavorText.casual: Weird, wonderful, and way more popular than they have any right to be. They look like they’re always smiling at you.
flavorText.pro: Requires cool, clean, well-cycled water (16–20°C). Sensitive to poor water quality and temperature spikes. Best kept alone or with similarly sized individuals.

hiddenLore: Axolotls are famous in Minecraft for their cute colors and healing powers. Searching 'minecraft' or 'cute' reveals their hidden pixelated aura.

ecology.comments: Tiny hitchhiker snail. Reproduces rapidly. Excellent detritus cleaner. Population self-regulates with food availability. Free cleanup crew!
Nearly indestructible plant. Great for fry and shrimp.
Red/green stem plant that colors up under strong light. Easy to propagate — just trim and replant tops. Great for color contrast.
Large, colorful snail with fun personality.
Burrowing snail that aerates substrate and eats leftover food. Mostly nocturnal. Livebearing — population can boom. Great for planted tanks.
Tiny floating plant that multiplies explosively. Great nutrient absorber but nearly impossible to eradicate once introduced. You have been warned.
Smallest crypt species. Very slow-growing carpeting plant. Needs patience — takes months to fill in. No CO2 required but helps.
Bulletproof epiphyte. Do not bury rhizome.
Large rosette plant. Heavy root feeder.
Slow-growing, nearly indestructible. Attach to hardscape — never bury rhizome. Prone to algae on leaves in high light.
Floating plant with round leaves and long roots. Provides shade and cover. Absorbs excess nutrients. Keep surface movement low.
Tall, grass-like background plant. Sends out runners rapidly. Very easy — just plant and watch it spread. Dislikes liquid carbon products.
Versatile crypt in green, red, and brown varieties. May melt when transplanted but recovers from roots. Low-tech staple.
Very tall background plant with ribbon-like leaves. Can reach the surface of deep tanks. Spreads aggressively via runners.
Grows upright in flame-like pattern. Unique texture for aquascaping. Attach to vertical surfaces for best effect.
Fast-growing stem plant with lacy leaves. Changes leaf shape depending on emersed/submersed growth. Excellent nutrient sponge.
Best algae-eating snail. Won't breed in freshwater.
Triangular frond pattern resembling Christmas tree branches. Great on mesh for walls or carpets. Slightly more demanding than Java Moss.
Slow-growing rheophyte with metallic sheen on leaves. Attach to hardscape like Anubias. Many rare and collectible varieties.
Flat spiral-shelled snail in red, blue, and leopard varieties. Can reproduce quickly — some see them as pests, others love them.
Nearly transparent, inexpensive shrimp. Great cleaners and fun to observe eating. Often sold as feeder shrimp but make great pets.
Upright stem plant with rounded leaves. Very undemanding. Can grow emersed. Also used as an herbal supplement (Brahmi).
Stunning dwarf cichlid from South America.
The red nose is a great indicator of water quality.
Massive, prehistoric surface predator. Jumps out of tanks — heavy, tight lid mandatory. Only for expert fishkeepers with enormous setups.
Larger rasbora with distinctive forked tail markings. Active swimmer needing a long tank. Hardy and peaceful.
Global record found via text search fallback.
Delicate nano rainbow with thread-like fin extensions. Peaceful and shy — keep with gentle tankmates only.
Global record found via text search fallback.
